source_name: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)
requirement: Ensure overall implementation provides an acceptable level of security as determined by the responsible authority in each agency or department.
evidence_quote: The responsible authority in each agency or department shall ensure that an overall implementation provides an acceptable level of security.

source_name: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)
requirement: Ensure that any module implementing key establishment capability is designed and built in a secure manner.
evidence_quote: It is the responsibility of the implementer to ensure that any module that implements a key establishment capability is designed and built in a secure manner.

source_name: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)
requirement: Guard against the disclosure of randomness used by parties, decapsulation keys, and shared secret keys to maintain security guarantees.
evidence_quote: Users shall, therefore, guard against the disclosure of these values [randomness used by the two parties, the decapsulation key, and the shared secret key itself].

source_name: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)
requirement: Employ cryptographic algorithms approved for protecting Federal Government-sensitive information in implementations complying with this standard.
evidence_quote: Implementations that comply with this standard shall employ cryptographic algorithms that have been approved for protecting Federal Government-sensitive information.

source_name: Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism Standard (ML-KEM)
requirement: Ensure exports of cryptographic modules implementing this standard comply with federal laws and are licensed by the Bureau of Industry and Security.
evidence_quote: Exports of cryptographic modules that implement this standard and technical data regarding them must comply with all federal laws and regulations and be licensed by the Bureau of Industry and Security of the U.S. Department of Commerce.

source_name: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA in X.509 PKI
requirement: Configure CA implementations to explicitly specify ML-DSA algorithms using the defined OIDs when encoding signatures in certificates and CRLs.
evidence_quote: Conforming Certification Authority (CA) implementations MUST specify the algorithms explicitly by using the OIDs specified in Section 2 when encoding ML-DSA signatures in certificates and CRLs.

source_name: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA in X.509 PKI
requirement: Ensure client implementations recognize the specific OIDs defined for ML-DSA when processing certificates and CRLs.
evidence_quote: Conforming client implementations that process certificates and CRLs using ML-DSA MUST recognize the corresponding OIDs.

source_name: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA in X.509 PKI
requirement: Enforce that the keyUsage extension in certificates containing ML-DSA public keys includes at least one of: digitalSignature, nonRepudiation, keyCertSign, or cRLSign.
evidence_quote: If the keyUsage extension is present in a certificate that includes id-ml-dsa-* ... then the subject public key can only be used for verifying digital signatures ... This means that the keyUsage extension MUST have at least one of the following bits set: digitalSignature, nonRepudiation, keyCertSign, cRLSign

source_name: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA in X.509 PKI
requirement: Prohibit the use of keyEncipherment, dataEncipherment, keyAgreement, encipherOnly, or decipherOnly bits in the keyUsage extension for certificates containing ML-DSA public keys.
evidence_quote: ML-DSA subject public keys cannot be used to establish keys or encrypt data, so the keyUsage extension MUST NOT have any of the following bits set: keyEncipherment, dataEncipherment, keyAgreement, encipherOnly, decipherOnly

source_name: Algorithm Identifiers for ML-DSA in X.509 PKI
requirement: Adopt the seed format for encoding ML-DSA private keys to efficiently store both private and public key components.
evidence_quote: The seed format is RECOMMENDED as it efficiently stores both the private and public key.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules
requirement: Validate cryptographic modules against FIPS 140-3 via accredited laboratories to ensure conformance before deployment.
evidence_quote: The Cryptographic Module Validation Program (CMVP) validates cryptographic modules to Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-3... In the CMVP, vendors of cryptographic modules use independent, accredited Cryptographic and Security Testing (CST) laboratories to have their modules tested.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules
requirement: Ensure cryptographic modules employ only approved security functions specified in FIPS or NIST Special Publications.
evidence_quote: Cryptographic modules that conform to this standard shall employ Approved security functions such as cryptographic algorithms, cryptographic key management techniques, and authentication techniques that have been approved for protecting Federal government sensitive information.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules
requirement: Develop acquisition plans for FIPS 140-3 compliant products and avoid using historical validation lists for procurement.
evidence_quote: Agencies should develop plans for the acquisition of products that are compliant with FIPS 140-3; however, agencies may purchase any of the products on the CMVP validated modules list. (Note: the CMVP Historical list should not be used for procurement decisions.)

source_name: FIPS 140-3 — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules
requirement: Assign responsibility to the operator and responsible authority for ensuring module security sufficiency and accepting residual risk.
evidence_quote: The operator of a cryptographic module is responsible for ensuring that the security provided by a module is sufficient and acceptable to the owner of the information that is being protected and that any residual risk is acknowledged and accepted. The responsible authority in each agency shall ensure that the security of the system is sufficient and acceptable.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules
requirement: Establish a review cycle of at least every five years to consider necessary updates or replacement of the standard.
evidence_quote: This standard will be reviewed at least every five years in order to consider necessary updates or replacement.

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Define security assurance requirements (SARs) to establish grounds for confidence that the TOE meets security functional requirements.
evidence_quote: assurance: grounds for confidence that a target of evaluation (TOE) meets the security functional requirements (SFRs)

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Utilize named sets of security assurance requirements (assurance packages) to standardize evaluation levels.
evidence_quote: assurance package: named set of security assurance requirements

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Identify and define the Target of Evaluation (TOE) boundaries to establish the scope of the security evaluation.
evidence_quote: establishes the core concept of a Target of Evaluation (TOE); describes the evaluation context

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Document the operational environment and different configurations of the TOE to ensure complete estate visibility.
evidence_quote: Different configurations of the TOE; Operational environment of the TOE

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Establish a Security Problem Definition (SPD) including threats and organizational security policies to guide the evaluation lifecycle.
evidence_quote: Security problem definition (SPD): General; Threats; Organizational security policies (OSPs); Assumptions

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Define the role and trust level of administrators who implement policies within the TOE security functionality.
evidence_quote: administrator: entity that has a level of trust with respect to all policies implemented by the TOE security functionality (TSF)

source_name: Common Criteria 2022 Part 1 — Introduction and General Model (R1)
requirement: Ensure evaluation results are meaningful by verifying the context of security properties and methods used.
evidence_quote: Evaluation authorities are advised to carefully check the products, properties, and methods to determine that an evaluation provides meaningful results.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Identify embedded validated modules by name, certificate number, and version in the Security Policy and validation test report.
evidence_quote: The FIPS 140-3 IUT submission (e.g., Security Policy and validation test report) shall precisely identify the EVM by name, CMVP certificate number and version(s).

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Mark all references to embedded module functionality with [EVM] in Security Policy tables to distinguish IUT from EVM services.
evidence_quote: This shall be done per below by marking '[EVM]' to the start of each applicable reference within the following WebCyptik tables... All other areas of the Security Policy where EVM information is needed that are not shown in the above tables, shall also be marked with [EVM].

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Ensure the embedded validated module (EVM) status is Active at the time of IUT submission to CMVP.
evidence_quote: The EVM status shall be Active (i.e., is not on the Historical or Revoked list) at the time of the IUT submission to the CMVP.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Inherit the Historical validation status if the embedded validated module moves to the historical list for any reason.
evidence_quote: The IUT shall inherit the Historical validation status if the EVM moves to the historical list for any reason (e.g., is sunset or an algorithm transition).

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Only claim EVM services or algorithms as approved if they were approved at the time of IUT submission.
evidence_quote: To claim an EVM's implementation as an approved service / algorithm within the IUT documentation, the IUT shall only use EVM services / algorithms that are approved at the time of the IUT submission to the CMVP

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Verify that the operational environment of the embedded algorithm implementation matches the module's tested environment.
evidence_quote: The operational environment under which the validated cryptographic algorithm implementation was tested by the CAVP must be identical to, or fully included in the operational environment that the cryptographic module is being tested under by the CST laboratory

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Ensure the embedded validated module is the same or higher security level as the IUT for all FIPS 140-3 sections except Mitigation of other attacks.
evidence_quote: For bound modules, the EVM shall be the same or higher security level as the IUT for all sections of the FIPS 140-3 standard, except for Mitigation of other attacks which can differ in security levels.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Re-validate algorithm implementations if the operational environment changes, such as moving from a 32-bit to a 64-bit platform.
evidence_quote: If an implementation has been tested on an X-bit processor (e.g. 32-bit, 64-bit), a claim cannot be made that the implementation also runs on different bit size processors... In this case the operational environments are not the same; therefore, the algorithm implementations must be re-tested

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Re-validate algorithm implementations if the operating system changes from the one tested during CAVP validation.
evidence_quote: If an algorithm implementation has been tested on one operating system, a claim cannot be made that the implementation also runs on another operating system when it is considered for module testing.

source_name: FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance for Post-Quantum Cryptography
requirement: Clearly distinguish IUT functionality from EVM functionality in the Security Policy and validation test report.
evidence_quote: The FIPS 140-3 Security Policy and validation test report for the IUT shall represent the functionality within its boundary with clear distinctions between IUT information and information associated with the EVM

source_name: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — Security and Privacy Controls (FISMA baseline)

source_name: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — Security and Privacy Controls (FISMA baseline)
requirement: Continuously monitor information systems to maintain confidence in security and privacy capabilities.
evidence_quote: ...establishing a comprehensive risk management program that uses the flexibility inherent in NIST publications to categorize systems, select and implement security and privacy controls that meet mission and business needs, assess the effectiveness of the controls, authorize the systems for operation, and continuously monitor the systems.

source_name: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 — Security and Privacy Controls (FISMA baseline)

requirement: Adopt hybrid cryptographic configurations that combine existing classical algorithms with Post-Quantum Cryptography to mitigate risks during the transition period.

source_name: Dubai Electronic Security Center — Post-Quantum Cryptography Guideline
requirement: Establish a documented Post-Quantum Cryptography guideline to prepare digital infrastructure against quantum threats.
evidence_quote: DESC launched the Post-Quantum Cryptography Guideline. This guide is designed to prepare Dubai's digital infrastructure to counter emerging quantum-based threats.

source_name: CISA PQC Product Category List (Updated per EO 14306)
requirement: Plan acquisitions to procure only PQC-capable products in categories where such products are widely available.
evidence_quote: When a particular category offers widely available PQC-capable products, organizations should plan acquisitions to procure only PQC-capable products from that category.

source_name: CISA PQC Product Category List (Updated per EO 14306)
requirement: Ensure products in transitioning categories implement PQC for core features and all secondary functionality like software updates.
evidence_quote: It is important that the products listed in Table 3 implement PQC for core features and for all secondary functionality, such as for software updates.

source_name: CISA PQC Product Category List (Updated per EO 14306)
requirement: Verify Endpoint Security products utilize PQC for relevant features such as firmware updates during procurement.
evidence_quote: When procuring, one needs to ensure that other relevant features of the product, such as firmware updates, are utilizing post-quantum cryptography (PQC).

source_name: Quantum-Safe Handbook and Quantum Readiness Index
requirement: Initiate quantum-safe transition planning with early resource allocation and cross-system coordination.
evidence_quote: The migration to quantum-safe solutions is a complex multi-year endeavour that needs early planning, resource allocation and coordination across systems, products, and processes.

source_name: Quantum-Safe Handbook and Quantum Readiness Index
requirement: Engage senior management to prioritize quantum-safe migration actions and secure buy-in for readiness steps.
evidence_quote: It also facilitates conversations with senior management and decision makers about the state of readiness to address quantum threat and can be used to seek buy-in on the recommended steps to strengthen their readiness.

source_name: ETSI EN 303 645 v2.1.1 — Cyber Security for Consumer IoT
requirement: Record a justification for each recommendation not applicable or not fulfilled by the consumer IoT device.
evidence_quote: Provision 4-1 A justification shall be recorded for each recommendation in the present document that is considered to be not applicable for or not fulfilled by the consumer IoT device.

source_name: ETSI EN 303 645 v2.1.1 — Cyber Security for Consumer IoT
requirement: Make a vulnerability disclosure policy publicly available including contact information and timelines for acknowledgement and status updates.
evidence_quote: Provision 5.2-1 The manufacturer shall make a vulnerability disclosure policy publicly available. This policy shall include, at a minimum: contact information for the reporting of issues; and information on timelines for: initial acknowledgement of receipt; and status updates until the resolution of the reported issues.
